description: Scoring rubric for social.plus docs pages. Both Claude (judge) and automated validators consume this. Update via PR; bumping version invalidates cached scores.

accuracy.description: Every API name, signature, type, parameter, and behavior claim matches the current SDK source.

levels.1: Contains demonstrably wrong API names, removed methods, or fabricated parameters.
levels.2: Mostly right but contains 2+ unverified or stale claims.
levels.3: One stale claim or one unverified signature.
levels.4: All API references verifiable against current SDK source; no fabrication.
levels.5: All API references verified AND page includes version/availability tags that match the SDK.

completeness.description: All public API surfaces this page is responsible for are documented; required parameters, return types, errors, and side effects are present. SDK function/reference pages that show code snippets include a Parameters section; overview/setup/concept pages do not need one unless they document SDK call inputs.

levels.1: Major public APIs missing from a page that claims to document them.
levels.2: Most APIs covered; several missing parameters, return types, or error cases.
levels.3: All APIs covered; some parameters lack descriptions or types.
levels.4: All APIs, parameters, return types, and primary error cases documented.
levels.5: Adds edge cases, rate limits, and side effects beyond the API signature.

cross_platform_parity.description: When a feature exists on multiple platforms (iOS / Android / TypeScript / Flutter), each platform has equivalent coverage: same sections, same depth, same terminology.

levels.1: Feature documented on only one platform despite being implemented in 2+.
levels.2: All platforms present but page lengths/depth differ by >3x.
levels.3: All platforms present; minor structural inconsistency.
levels.4: Same section structure across platforms; equivalent depth.
levels.5: Parity matrix shows no gaps AND examples follow each platform's idiom (not transliterated).

examples.description: Code examples are runnable, idiomatic for the platform, use current API names, and demonstrate the actual task.

levels.1: Examples reference removed APIs or won't compile.
levels.2: Examples compile but use deprecated patterns or wrong idioms.
levels.3: Examples compile and use current APIs but are toy / overly contrived.
levels.4: Examples are realistic, current, and platform-idiomatic.
levels.5: Examples are realistic AND include error handling, AND are extractable as standalone snippets.

clarity.description: Plain language. Defines its terms. No marketing fluff. Right-sized for an engineer scanning, not reading.

levels.1: Marketing copy where reference is needed; unexplained jargon.
levels.2: Mixed registers; some sections explain, some only assert.
levels.3: Clear but verbose; could be cut 20% without loss.
levels.4: Tight, terms defined on first use, scan-friendly headings.
levels.5: Tight AND opens with a one-sentence answer to 'what does this page give me'.

ai_consumability.description: Page is structured so an AI agent (customer's or ours via MCP) can extract correct answers without ambiguity. SDK language snippets are grouped with CodeGroup, not nested in CardGroup; CardGroup is reserved for navigation and related-topic cards.

levels.1: No frontmatter; ambiguous title; terms drift from glossary.
levels.2: Frontmatter present but title/description don't front-load intent.
levels.3: Good frontmatter; uses glossary terms; some snippets not extractable (interleaved with prose).
levels.4: Frontmatter front-loads intent; glossary terms used consistently; snippets are extractable.
levels.5: All of #4 PLUS canonical anchors for every public API mentioned PLUS appears correctly in llms.txt.

A.description: Fully autonomous. AI generates and merges the fix; humans approve the policy once, not the diff. Gate: eval score does not regress AND validators pass.
examples: Code snippet that fails static validation → regenerate from canonical example
Missing platform variant stub generated from SDK symbol graph

B.description: AI generates the change; a human rubber-stamps a batch (e.g., 50 PRs in one session), not individual diffs.
examples: Section reorder to match house structure
Filling in undocumented parameters from SDK type info
Cross-platform parity gap-fill using a complete platform as template

C.description: Human-led. AI preps the work — surfaces the question, drafts options, links relevant code — but the decision is human.

golden_pages.description: Pages tagged golden always get per-change human review regardless of tier. Add via PR.
